parties: Between Dwight Ericson, Petitioner, and The Deputy Director of Correction Services of Canada: Pacific Region, and The Director of Kent Institution, Respondents
citation: IN THE MATTER OF Dwight Ericson having been transferred from Elbow Lake Institution by Reasons of Involuntary Transfers, Authorized by the Correction Service of Canada: Pacific Region, on October 16, 1991 and presently in the Custody of The Director of Kent Institution. AND IN THE MATTER OF An Application for a Writ of Habeas Corpus Ad Subjiciendum and for a Writ of Certiorari in Aid Thereof
court: British Columbia Supreme Court Vancouver, British Columbia Spencer J.
body_html:
Heard: November 13, 1991
Judgment: November 14, 1991
(8 pp.)


Counsel for the Petitioner: Derek C. LaCroix.
Counsel for the Respondents: Linda Ostry.

SPENCER J.:— This is an application for habeas corpus with certiorari in aid to quash the decision of the Warden of the Elbow Lake Correctional Institute to transfer the petitioner to a more secure penal institution. According to the evidence before me the petitioner is serving a five year sentence for two counts of the possession of stolen property. His sentence began in November 1990. In February 1991 he was sent to the Elbow Lake Institution, a level 2 minimum security establishment. He has had a good record of work and behaviour while incarcerated. In particular, he has been able to receive many visits from his family and friends there, has enjoyed a number of social and work related passes and has become involved in writing a script for and advising a film company with respect to a film series about crime. He has the opportunity to pursue his writing with that company after his eventual release. His transfer to the Matsqui Institution, a level 5 institution, has interrupted that work opportunity, has generally subjected him to a greater degree of security while incarcerated and has reduced the privileges available to him.

At the heart of the petitioner's complaint is the allegation that he was transferred without being given an opportunity to be heard in opposition to the charges levelled against him that caused his transfer. All he was told was that he was named by several inmates at Elbow Lake as the person responsible for the traffic of narcotics within that institution and that therefore he, together with others, was being transferred to other prisons. Without knowing any details of that allegation he strenuously denied it. His denial was rejected and his transfer was confirmed.

Any review of a decision by the custodial authorities with respect to the place where a prisoner should be held and the prison discipline that should be imposed upon him must proceed cautiously, in the perspective that those who run a prison are in a peculiarly advantageous position to know what is happening there and to assess its significance. In particular, where, as here, the authority says that to give further details of the allegations against an inmate would reveal the source of confidential communications and expose another inmate to the risk of retaliation, that opinion should not lightly be rejected. At the same time, it must be remembered that the courts have jurisdiction over these questions in a broad sense and a mandate to ensure that the prison authority observes the rules of procedural fairness, see R. vs. Miller [1985] 49 C.R. (3d) 1 (S.C.C.) and Cardinal and Oswald vs. Director of Kent Institution [1985] 49 C.R. (3d) 35 (S.C.C.). Counsel for the respondents concedes as much and opposed this petition solely on its facts, saying that as much as could be done without jeopardizing the safety of informants has been done.

I find nothing flawed in the way in which the respondents reacted to what appeared to be an emergency situation in the Elbow Lake Institution by transferring the petitioner out pending a further enquiry. There was evidence of an increasing drug problem in the institution, marked by incidents of overdose by two inmates and by the accidental death of a third. A number of inmates anonymously named the petitioner as the organizer of trafficking into the institution and he was therefore transferred. All he was told was this:

"Information has been received from several different sources that you are trafficking in drugs in Elbow Lake Institution. Your case will be reviewed for reclassification and/or return to Elbow Lake Institution upon completion of our investigation." Upon receipt of that notice the petitioner denied the allegation.

According to the evidence there then followed three investigations, one by the authorities at Elbow Lake, one by an official from another penal institution and one by the R.C.M.P. As a result, the petitioner was given another notice which read:

"Your involvement in the drug trade at ELI has been identified by several inmate sources. Given this information and subsequent information that the appropriate inmates have been removed from the institution lead (sic) to the conclusion that you present too great a risk to remain in a minimum security environment. You were initially transferred to Kent Institution pending the outcome of an investigation and until bed space could be arranged."

The effect of that notice was to confirm his transfer away from Elbow Lake and the consequent loss of some of the privileges he enjoyed there.

There is also evidence that in an interview with one of the Elbow Lake staff, the petitioner was told words to the effect that he had been named by other inmates as the "main man" of the drug trafficking there.

It is immediately apparent that the petitioner, while denying the allegations in a general sense, had no idea of the identity of those who named his as a trafficker or of the nature of their evidence against him. I accept that to name the informants, other inmates of the penal system, might well expose them to the risk of retaliation and injury. The prison staff owes a duty to protect them. I accept also that the staff, who know the precise nature of the allegations made against the petitioner, are in the best position to judge what detail may and may not be given to the petitioner without jeopardizing the safety of those other inmates. But the court has a role to play in ensuring that the petitioner has been fairly treated, and fair treatment requires as much as possible that he know some detail of the charges against him so that he can respond to them. Otherwise he finds himself caught in the strange world described, in its extreme, by Franz Kafka in "The Trial" (1925) see Library of Congress No. 68-59195.

Here, the petitioner knew he was alleged to be a leader of the prison drug trade. He has no record of conviction in any drug related matter nor of any prior disciplinary proceeding in the prison system. His denial of any involvement in the allegations against him is responded to in these words by the Preventative Security Co-Ordinator who interviewed him during the review of the decision to transfer him:

"I offered the petitioner the opportunity to verbally respond to the allegations. He denied the allegations but did not provide further explanation, indicating that the allegations were "preposterous" and stating "you'll hear from my lawyer"."

Unless the petitioner were given some detail of what had been said against him it is difficult to imagine what further explanation he might have given.

The prison authorities are undoubtedly faced with a very delicate task in deciding what detail can safely be given to a prisoner such as the petitioner without jeopardizing the safety of others. I am not privy to what the respondents know to make that judgment myself. Nor am I given the role of deciding whether the decision reached by the respondents on the merits of the case was right or wrong. My task is simply to ensure that the petitioner was fairly treated.

With all due deference to the respondents, I cannot think that the petitioner could not have been given greater detail here. If other inmates have informed against him, it is reasonable to suppose they did not implicate themselves in any wrongdoing. So, probably, their evidence was hearsay. If they informed about the petitioner's reputation within the institution they could have nothing to say that would identify them. If the reputation they spoke of identified the petitioner with any specific activity he should have been given that detail so that he would know from what it was he must defend himself. I think it unlikely that a drug trafficking operation within a prison is so devoid of system that specific allegations cannot be put to a suspect. I incline to the view expressed by Smith J. in Balian v. Regional Transfer Board et al. (1988), 62 C.R. (3d) 258 at 266, "that experience has shown that a great deal can be disclosed while protecting the identity of fellow inmates who inform". I am not convinced that in this case the convergence of evidence from eight inmates, otherwise said to be unrelated to each other, and the coinciding findings of the three separate investigations points so inexorably to the petitioner's guilt that he must have been guilty. All eight inmates may have listened to the same source. All three investigators may have interviewed the same informants. There is no way of telling. What is clear is that the petitioner was not permitted to play a reasonably informed part in the whole process. That was procedurally unfair to him.

Counsel for the respondents sought to rely on the decision of the Federal Court of Appeal in Gallant v. Correctional Service Canada (Pacific Region) (1989), 92 N.R. 292. The court there, at page 301, drew a distinction between the case of a single infraction leading to a particular punishment, the details of which should be given to an inmate to permit him a chance to answer it, and a continuing series of wrongdoings that leads to a transfer of the inmate for the sake of the orderly and proper administration of the institution. I must confess to some difficulty in appreciating the difference, but in any event what was done here was done as a result of a single endeavour alleged against the petitioner, not the result of "all the wrongdoings" referred to in that case. Certainly it was done with a view to protecting the integrity of the Elbow Lake Institution as a whole, but the petitioner was singled out for that purpose without being given a real opportunity to defend himself.

I wish to emphasize that I do not denigrate the role of the prison authorities in holding the delicate balance between the right of the individual inmate to fair treatment and the rights of informants to be kept secret and secure. Nor do I diminish in any sense the very heavy responsibility laid upon them to administer a difficult community. I simply say that the almost total absence of detail supplied to the petitioner of the allegations against him, which lead to a very significant change in his custodial status and privileges and in his opportunities for rehabilitation persuade me that his petition should be granted. There will be an order accordingly to quash the decision of the Warden of the Elbow Lake Institution and an order that he be returned either there, or to some other appropriate institution with the same security classification.

SPENCER J.
